<!DOCTYPE PubmedArticleSet PUBLIC "-//NLM//DTD PubMedArticle, 1st January 2025//EN" "https://dtd.nlm.nih.gov/ncbi/pubmed/out/pubmed_250101.dtd">
<PubmedArticleSet>
<PubmedArticle><MedlineCitation Status="Publisher" Owner="NLM"><PMID Version="1">42435526</PMID><DateRevised><Year>2026</Year><Month>07</Month><Day>11</Day></DateRevised><Article PubModel="Print-Electronic"><Journal><ISSN IssnType="Electronic">1618-095X</ISSN><JournalIssue CitedMedium="Internet"><Volume>159</Volume><PubDate><Year>2026</Year><Month>Jun</Month><Day>29</Day></PubDate></JournalIssue><Title>Phytomedicine : international journal of phytotherapy and phytopharmacology</Title><ISOAbbreviation>Phytomedicine</ISOAbbreviation></Journal><ArticleTitle>Identification of anti-fibrotic components of Bofutsushosan under conditions of age-associated obesity in mice.</ArticleTitle><Pagination><StartPage>158522</StartPage><MedlinePgn>158522</MedlinePgn></Pagination><ELocationID EIdType="doi" ValidYN="Y">10.1016/j.phymed.2026.158522</ELocationID><ELocationID EIdType="pii" ValidYN="Y">S0944-7113(26)00755-5</ELocationID><Abstract><AbstractText Label="BACKGROUND" NlmCategory="BACKGROUND">Bofutsushosan (BTS) is a traditional herbal medicine used to reduce body weight, edema, and constipation. Aged individuals often present pro-fibrotic comorbidities such as metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), chronic kidney disease, and heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. Although the weight-reducing effect of BTS is well recognized, its effects under aged conditions remain uncharacterized.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="PURPOSE" NlmCategory="OBJECTIVE">To characterize the anti-fibrotic effects of BTS under age-associated obesity and identify the components responsible.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="STUDY DESIGN" NlmCategory="METHODS">An aged diet-induced obese mouse model combined with an in vitro TGFβ-induced fibrosis model in 3T3-L1 preadipocytes, followed by component-resolved screening and reformulation of BTS.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="METHODS" NlmCategory="METHODS">63-week-old mice were fed a high-fat diet containing 2% BTS for 2 months, and tissues were analyzed by histology and qPCR. In 3T3-L1 preadipocytes, pro-fibrotic gene expression and SMAD2/3 phosphorylation were assessed following treatment with whole BTS, single-component-depleted BTS, or individual components.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="RESULTS" NlmCategory="RESULTS">BTS reduced body weight by 14.8% with decreased food intake, reduced adipose tissue size, and ameliorated hepatic steatosis. Pro-fibrotic genes were reduced in adipose tissue and liver, including 60.6% and 46.2% reductions of Col1a1. BTS suppressed TGFβ-mediated pro-fibrotic gene expression and SMAD2/3 phosphorylation in 3T3-L1 preadipocytes. Among the 18 components, Sal Mirabilis was the primary pro-fibrotic constituent, whereas Forsythiae Fructus, Zingiberis Rhizoma, Rhei Rhizoma, and Scutellariae Radix were anti-fibrotic. A modified formula lacking Sal Mirabilis and supplemented with these four ingredients exerted a stronger anti-fibrotic effect.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="CONCLUSION" NlmCategory="CONCLUSIONS">BTS exerts anti-fibrotic effects under age-associated obesity, and its therapeutic potential can be enhanced by removing pro-fibrotic and increasing anti-fibrotic components.</AbstractText><CopyrightInformation>Copyright © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier GmbH..
